sources.name: Sonic Physics Guide (SPG) — Jumping
sources.notes: Confirmed: jump_force 6.5 px/frame (1664 sub-pixels), Knuckles 6.0 px/frame. Gravity 0.21875 px/frame² (56 sub-pixels). Variable height: release caps Y at -4 px/frame.

sources.name: Sonic Physics Guide (SPG) — Running
sources.notes: Core running constants: ACC 0.046875, DEC 0.5, FRC 0.046875, top speed 6.0 px/frame. SPG notes Sonic 2+ behavior difference when Ground Speed exceeds top_speed.

sources.name: Sonic Retro SPG — general
sources.notes: All 256-subpixel hex values for physics constants. Primary authoritative source for Genesis Sonic physics.

notes: Sonic 2 uses the same core physics engine as Sonic 1 (same acc/dec/friction/top_speed/gravity/jump constants).
Key SPG confirmation: 'The source does not differentiate physics constants between Sonic 1 vs 2 vs 3 — it treats them as unified across the classic era.'
Sonic 2 additions: Super Sonic, Miles 'Tails' Prower as second player, Super Spin Dash (new move).
The Spin Dash is NEW in Sonic 2 — not present in Sonic 1. It allows charging a spin in place before launching.
256 sub-pixels per pixel (8.8 fixed-point), same as Sonic 1.
All physics values that match Sonic 1 are marked as confirmed from Sonic 1 SPG documentation (same engine).
sonic_1_vs_sonic_2_differences: Tails (Miles Prower) playable in multiplayer — can fly using twin tails
Running mechanic: Sonic 2+ does not apply acceleration_speed when Ground Speed > top_speed (Sonic 1 does still apply it)
Some stages have unique mechanics: Casino Night (pinball), Aquatic Ruin (water physics)

subpixel_system.description: Same 256 sub-pixel system as Sonic 1. All physics constants expressed in sub-pixels in the SPG.

display.notes: Genesis displays at 320×224 (wide mode) vs NES 256×240. Stages appear wider as a result.

ground_movement._source_note: Core constants identical to Sonic 1. Confirmed by SPG which documents these as unified across classic Sonic.

deceleration.notes: Applied when pressing opposite direction while moving.

friction.notes: Applied when no direction is pressed.

sonic_2_behavior_note.description: In Sonic 2+, when Ground Speed > top_speed, acceleration is NOT applied. In Sonic 1, acceleration IS still applied above top speed.
sonic_2_behavior_note.significance: medium — affects how Sonic 2 handles speed obtained from slopes or springs exceeding top_speed

spin_dash.description: Crouch (Down) + press jump to charge a spin in place. Release or press jump again to launch.

charged_release_speed.notes: Release speed depends on charge level (number of button presses while crouching). At full charge, launch speed significantly exceeds normal top speed.

notes: Spin Dash allows instant acceleration from stationary to high speed.
Critical for speedrunning: replaces the need for a run-up before slopes.
Changes level design possibilities: previously, hills required a run-up to gain momentum. Spin Dash makes any incline a launch ramp.

jump._source_note: Same jump constants as Sonic 1 — confirmed by SPG treating classic series as unified.

jump_force.notes: Force applied at jump initiation along the ground angle normal (not straight up — slope-aware).

variable_jump.mechanic: Releasing jump caps upward Y Speed at -4 px/frame before gravity is applied that frame
variable_jump.notes: This means: short tap produces a truncated arc; holding produces full height arc.

slope_aware_jump.description: Jump force is applied via: X Speed -= jump_force * sin(Ground Angle); Y Speed -= jump_force * cos(Ground Angle). Jumping off a slope sends Sonic in the slope-normal direction.

gravity._source_note: Same as Sonic 1 — confirmed by SPG.

air_drag.condition: Active when Y Speed < 0 (ascending) AND |X Speed| > 0.125
air_drag.notes: Air drag only acts during the ascending half of the jump arc and only when moving quickly horizontally. Slows horizontal speed slightly on ascent.

fly_mechanic.description: Tails can fly by pressing jump in the air — his twin tails spin to generate lift.
notes: Flying maintains a slow upward or hovering movement.
Flight depletes — Tails gets tired after several seconds and descends.
In Sonic 2, Tails is a second player following Sonic and can be flown out-of-bounds.
In Sonic 3, Tails is more fully featured as a solo character.

super_sonic.activation: Jump while holding nothing (releases collected rings)
notes: Super Sonic moves significantly faster — approximate 2x multiplier on top speed.
Invincible to all enemies. Destroys enemies on contact.
Requires rings to maintain — drains 1 ring/second. Reverts to normal when rings hit 0.

water_physics._note: Water stages present in Aquatic Ruin zone.
water_physics.speed_reduction: yes, but exact values need verification
water_physics.notes: Same physics principles as Sonic 1 water (Labyrinth Zone) but specific values for S2 need ROM verification.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: Spin Dash fundamentally changes the level design contract. In Sonic 1, you needed a run-up to achieve high speed. In Sonic 2, any flat surface + spin dash = instant momentum. Level designers for Sonic 2 can place speed sections anywhere, not just after long flat corridors.

The core physics are identical to Sonic 1 (same constants) but the Spin Dash's instant acceleration changes how the physics FEEL — the game is faster and more responsive even though the underlying physics engine is unchanged.

Tails' flight ability in Sonic 2 is primarily a multiplayer accessibility tool — the second player can fly over obstacles that the first player must run through. This creates interesting cooperative dynamics.

Super Sonic as an endgame bonus speed mode changes the game's late-game difficulty curve: players who find all emeralds get a mode that trivializes most obstacles. The challenge becomes ring management, not obstacle avoidance.
